title: Migrating Birds
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Bird migration is (0) one of nature's most remarkable phenomena, with billions of birds travelling thousands of kilometres each year between their breeding and wintering grounds. This incredible journey has fascinated scientists and birdwatchers for centuries.||Birds migrate primarily (9) to find food and suitable breeding conditions. In autumn, as temperatures drop and food becomes scarce in northern regions, birds fly south (10) to warmer areas where resources are more abundant. The Arctic Tern holds the record for the longest migration, travelling (11) from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back each year, covering over 70,000 kilometres.||Scientists are still learning (12) about how birds navigate during their journeys. It appears that birds use multiple methods, including the sun, stars, and Earth's magnetic field. Some species (13) can even detect polarised light, which helps them orient themselves. Young birds often make their first migration (14) without their parents, relying entirely on instinct.||Climate change is affecting migration patterns, (15) with some species arriving at their destinations earlier each year. Habitat loss along migration routes (16) has also made these journeys more dangerous, as birds struggle to find suitable places to rest and refuel.

example.text: Bird migration is (0) of nature's most remarkable phenomena.

questions.explanation: Purpose infinitive: 'migrate to find' indicates the goal of migration.
Preposition 'to' indicates direction: 'fly south to warmer areas'.
Expression 'from X to Y' indicates the journey from one place to another.
Verb 'learning about' means to learn about a subject.
Modal verb 'can' expresses ability or capability.
Preposition 'without' indicates the absence of parents.
Preposition 'with' introduces additional information about the change.
Present perfect: 'has made' indicates the effect up to the present.
